subject: Lange v. California (2021) and warrantless home entry in pursuit of a fleeing misdemeanor suspect
central_question: A police officer tries to pull you over for something minor, and you are already home — pulling into your own garage. Can he stick his foot under the closing door and follow you inside, without a warrant?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands why the Supreme Court in 2021 refused to give police an automatic right to chase a fleeing misdemeanor suspect into the home, and why the home remains the hardest place for the state to enter.
surprise: Police had argued that chasing anyone who flees — even over a honking ticket — automatically lets them enter your home without a warrant; a unanimous Supreme Court said no, not automatically.
stakes: Whether the most protected place in American law — your home — loses its protection the moment a police officer decides to chase you over something minor.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in policing, privacy, the home and how systems work
demand_evidence: 'Can a cop follow you into your home?' is a recognizable, self-relevant rights question; everyday traffic-stop/home scenarios are a huge interest area.
Recent (2021), advertiser-safe, evergreen; a vivid foot-under-the-garage-door fact pattern.
competition: Coverage is mostly legal/news; few give a neutral 'why the home is special + case-by-case' explainer.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant (your garage, your front door); neutral; the closing-arc episode on 'the home as the last fortress' after the EP6-13 search/track/take/speech/DNA cases.
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (594 U.S. 295); Cornell LII and Justia document the record.
visual_feasibility: High: a garage door descending and a foot stopping it; a 'home = strongest protection' fortress motif; a sliding scale of minor-to-serious offenses.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2021), unanimous in judgment; opinion public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Arthur Lange (DUI, public record) described neutrally; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).

Vote framing: say 'unanimous in the judgment / 9-0 to vacate and remand', NOT 'unanimous opinion' — Roberts (joined Alito) concurred only in the judgment and favored a broader hot-pursuit entry rule. Present both rationales fairly (home privacy + case-by-case vs officer safety + preventing escape).
The legal question was about misdemeanor FLIGHT, not the DUI specifically; the DUI is what the entry revealed. Keep neutral on policing.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
